statement.markdown:
Given an integer array `arr` and an integer `k`, return the number of good subarrays. A good subarray is defined as a subarray that has at most `k` distinct integers.

A subarray is a contiguous part of an array. An integer is considered to be a part of a subarray if it appears in that subarray.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer `n`, the length of the array.
The second line contains `n` space-separated integers representing the array `arr`.
The third line contains an integer `k`.

constraints.description: The maximum number of distinct integers.

hints.text: Consider using a sliding window approach to track subarrays with at most k distinct integers.
You will need a data structure to efficiently count and update the number of distinct integers within the current window.
Think about how you can use a frequency map in conjunction with the sliding window to solve this problem.

editorial.title: Subarrays with K Different Integers
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by counting the number of subarrays with at most k distinct integers and then subtracting the number of subarrays with at most k-1 distinct integers. This approach works because it effectively isolates the subarrays with exactly k distinct integers.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subarrays and checking each one for the number of distinct integers. This approach is correct but has a time complexity of O(n^2) due to the nested loops required to generate all subarrays and count distinct integers in each one.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using a sliding window technique with two pointers to track the subarrays with at most k distinct integers.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a single pass through the array, maintaining a count of distinct integers within the current window. When the number of distinct integers exceeds k, the window is shrunk from the left until it meets the condition again. The number of subarrays ending at each position is then added to the result.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that it accurately counts the number of subarrays with at most k distinct integers. By subtracting the count of subarrays with at most k-1 distinct integers, we effectively isolate the subarrays with exactly k distinct integers. The time complexity of O(n) and space complexity of O(n) are achieved due to the single pass through the array and the use of a count dictionary.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the solution with sample inputs, such as n = 5, arr = [1, 2, 1, 2, 3], and k = 2, helps verify its correctness.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly assuming that the problem requires a more complex data structure, such as a segment tree or a trie.
Failing to consider the edge case where k is 1, which requires special handling.
Not accounting for the fact that the sliding window approach can be used to solve the problem efficiently.
Incorrectly calculating the number of subarrays with at most k distinct integers.
Not subtracting the count of subarrays with at most k-1 distinct integers to isolate the subarrays with exactly k distinct integers.
